patches_submitted.subject: [RFC PATCH 0/3] mm/numa: reserve standby NUMA nodes for runtime claiming

version_history.subject: [RFC PATCH 0/3] mm/numa: reserve standby NUMA nodes for runtime claiming

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 1/3] mm/numa: add exclusive node pool and numa=standby boot parameter
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new feature to the Linux kernel's NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) subsystem, allowing for the creation of 'standby' nodes at boot time. These standby nodes can be claimed by drivers at runtime, enabling them to place hotplugged memory on distinct NUMA nodes without relying on BIOS-assigned proximity domains. The patch includes a new exclusive node pool and a claim/release interface for drivers to use these standby nodes. It also updates the NUMA distance table to include entries for standby nodes. This change allows for more flexibility in memory tier membership and fallback ordering, but raises concerns about dynamic addition of nodes and potential ambiguity with phys_to_target_node().

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 2/3] acpi/numa: add CONFIG_ACPI_NUMA_STANDBY_NODES
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new configuration option, CONFIG_ACPI_NUMA_STANDBY_NODES, which allows platforms to reserve empty NUMA nodes at boot for later use by drivers in memory tiering or isolation scenarios. The number of standby nodes to reserve is configurable and can be set between 0 and 16. This feature defers the creation of standby nodes until after NUMA emulation runs, ensuring consistency with existing numbering behaviors. Drivers can then claim these reserved nodes using numa_request_exclusive_node().

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 3/3] acpi/numa: add CONFIG_ACPI_NUMA_ADD_CFMWS_NODES
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new configuration option, CONFIG_ACPI_NUMA_ADD_CFMWS_NODES, which allows the number of additional standby NUMA nodes to be reserved per CEDT CFMWS entry. This is useful for CXL drivers that want to place memory on distinct NUMA nodes within the same CXL Fixed Memory Window. The patch also updates the acpi_parse_cfmws function to request any standby nodes created after numa_emulation runs. The number of additional standby nodes to reserve can be set between 0 and 4, with a default value of 0.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces the ability to reserve empty 'standby' NUMA nodes at boot, allowing drivers to place hotplugged memory on distinct nodes later at runtime without relying on BIOS-provided topology. The reserved nodes are initially offline but can be brought online by a driver and released when done. Three methods are provided to reserve standby nodes: a boot parameter, two kernel configuration options, and the ability to reserve nodes per CXL Fixed Memory Window. The patch series includes testing under various scenarios, including QEMU emulation and fallback cases.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM)
Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM)
Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM)
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch series introduces Private Memory Nodes, which allows for isolation of memory allocation on a per-node basis. The goal is to enable features like compressed RAM and improve system resource management. However, the patch also raises questions about how to limit page allocations to folio allocations or disallow slab allocations on private nodes. It appears that implementing such restrictions would be complex and may not provide significant benefits.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer David requested allowing only folio allocations from private memory nodes, citing that page allocations are written in terms of page allocations and rewriting interfaces would introduce boilerplate code.

Reviewer David expressed concerns about the feasibility of disallowing page allocation and limiting it to folios, citing that folio allocations are written in terms of page allocations and would require significant boilerplate changes. He also suggested considering alternative approaches such as providing more context to the allocator or using the memalloc_* interface.

Reviewer David (Arm) noted that the current implementation of private memory nodes does not differentiate between folio and page allocations, as both ultimately call __get_pages_from_freelist(), which does not care about the distinction. He suggested adding a flag to allow slab allocations on private nodes with __GFP_THISNODE.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that implementing a separate allocation path for private memory nodes would be too invasive and instead proposes using the existing __GFP_THISNODE flag to allow slab allocations on the private node, while also providing a new userland driver (anondax) to test the functionality without specialty hardware.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addresses David's concern about the inability to prevent hugetlb from spilling into private nodes, explaining that MEMALLOC_PRIVATE is still needed for this purpose and can be repurposed for Brendan's needs as well.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ok, this was easier to test than I expected, and hugetlb is indeed a
stickler.  We can't get there 100% with just MEMALLOC_FOLIO, we still
need a MEMALLOC_PRIVATE - specifically because of users like hugetlb.

hugetlb uses __GFP_THISNODE to do its allocations, and all hugetlb
allocations are folio allocations - so the code you shared by itself
does not gate hugetlb from spilling into private nodes.

That means we still need something like this in hugetlb:

  if (node_is_private(nid))
      /* fail allocation */

HOWEVER... if you have MEMALLOC_PRIVATE - you make the allocation
failure a *page allocator* problem, and it serves exactly the same
purpose that __GFP_PRIVATE did.

the resulting code is two lines in my anondax driver:

    unsigned int priv_flags = memalloc_private_save();
    ret = do_anonymous_page_node(vmf, dev_dax->target_node);
    memalloc_private_restore(priv_flags);

No special hugetlb, slab, arch code handling - they all just fail
to allocate / fall back.  If they fail - it means that code is using
a bad nodemask and we need to go fix it (exactly what we want!)

I think additionally, we might be able to repurpose MEMALLOC_PRIVATE
flag for Brendan's needs as well [1].

Their goal (IIRC) was to have a pile of unmapped blocks that could
be opportunistically converted to normal memory, but otherwise left
unmapped and sitting in the buddy.

Same thing - different filter point (blocks vs nodes).

If you set MEMALLOC_PRIVATE - it makes private node allocations
possible, and "private block" access (without conversion) possible.

Otherwise private nodes are unreachable, and private blocks would be
treated like CMA (last-resort stealing, lazy-direct-mapping).

And they stack (private blocks on private nodes :V).

I don't have enough time looking at his proposal, but it seems like we
can kill two birds with one stone on this.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/agYJcRgOHho8upVv@gourry-fedora-PF4VCD3F/

~Gregory

review_comments.raw_body:

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author Gregory Price is addressing David's (Arm) feedback on the patch, specifically concerns about locking and isolation in private memory nodes. He acknowledges that he didn't fully understand the request initially but provides a detailed explanation of how __GFP_THISNODE works under the hood, including its interaction with slab allocation and page allocator flags. He concludes that without explicit filtering, the mechanism is fragile and proposes two possible solutions for mm/slub.c to either refuse slab allocations on private nodes or not upgrade private-node slab requests with __GFP_THISNODE.
review_comments.raw_body:
So, I remember this being asked, and I didn't fully grok the request.

I'm still not sure I fully understand the question, so apologies if I'm
answer the wrong things here.

I understand this question in two ways:

  1) Can we disallow PAGE allocation and limit this to FOLIO allocation
  2) Can we disallow [Feature] (i.e. slab) allocation targeting the node.


1) Can we disallow page allocation and limit this to folios?

No, I don't think so.

Folio allocations are written in terms of page allocations, we would
have to rewrite folio allocation interfaces and introduce a bunch of
boilerplate for the sake of this.

struct page *__alloc_pages_noprof(gfp_t gfp, unsigned int order,
                int preferred_nid, nodemask_t *nodemask)
{
        struct page *page;

        page = __alloc_frozen_pages_noprof(gfp, order, preferred_nid, nodemask);
        if (page)
                set_page_refcounted(page);
        return page;
}

struct folio *__folio_alloc_noprof(gfp_t gfp, unsigned int order, int preferred_nid,
                nodemask_t *nodemask)
{
        struct page *page = __alloc_pages_noprof(gfp | __GFP_COMP, order,
                                        preferred_nid, nodemask);
	return page_rmappable_folio(page);
}

At the end of the day, this all reduces to `get_pages_from_freelist`,
and at that level we don't really care about folio vs page.

__GFP_COMP is insufficient to differentiate between a non-folio compound
page and a folio, and __GFP_COMP is passed into __alloc_pages_*
interfaces all over the kernel.

Trying to detach these paths things seems like a horrible rats nest /
not feasible / will create a lot of boilerplate for little value.

(I did not fully understand this request when it was asked, I do
 not fully understand this request not, please let me know if I
 have misunderstood what you were asking).



2) Can we disallow SLAB allocation.

Yeah, but I think a better question is whether there's a difference
between alloc_pages_node() and kmalloc_node() when it all just sinks
to the same fundamental code in mm/page_alloc.c

Maybe there's an argument for something like NP_OPT_KMALLOC (allow slab
allocations on the private node w/ __GFP_THISNODE)

On my current set, I don't implement any explicit filtering at all in
mm/page_alloc.c - the filtering is a function of the nodes not being
present in the FALLBACK list and only having a NOFALLBACK list.

What __GFP_THISNODE actually does under the hood is just switch
which zone list (FALLBACK vs NOFALLBACK) is used for the target node.

For isolation w/o __GFP_PRIVATE, we're removing N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes
from *their own FALLBACK* list and only adding them to their NOFALLBACK
list.  That means to reach a private node you MUST use __GFP_THISNODE.

I realize this is confusing, but essentially we don't have to modify
mm/page_alloc.c to get the __GFP_THISNODE filtering, we get this from
the fallback/nofallback list construction.


Ok, so how does this flush out in practice - and why do I call this
filtering mechanism fragile?

consider kmalloc_node() and __slab_alloc():

kmalloc_node(...)
  \u2514\u2500 ___slab_alloc()     mm/slub.c:4406   pc.flags |= __GFP_THISNODE
      \u2514\u2500 new_slab(s, pc.flags, node)
          \u2514\u2500 allocate_slab(s, flags, node)
              \u2514\u2500 alloc_slab_page(flags, node, oo, \u2026)
                  \u2514\u2500 __alloc_frozen_pages(flags, order, node, NULL);

Slab silently upgrades the page allocator flags here to include
__GFP_THISNODE - even if the user didn't request that behavior.

This is exactly the kind of "spillage" I said was hard to police at LSF.

Without __GFP_PRIVATE, we have to keep an eye on what around the kernel
is using __GFP_THISNODE and how.

For mm/slub.c we can choose to do one of thwo things

  1) 100% refuse slab allocations on private nodes, i.e.:

     kmalloc_node(..., private_nid, __GFP_THISNODE)

     And will fail (return NULL).

  or

  2) Do not upgrade private-node slab requests w/ __GFP_THISNODE
     
     This allows kmalloc_node() to work the same as folio_alloc()
     or alloc_pages() interfaces (__GFP_THISNODE is the key), with
     the understanding that any __GFP_THISNODE user

We can opt these nodes into slab/kmalloc with a NP_OPT_SLAB
if the owner wants kmalloc_node(), with the understanding that any
caller using __GFP_THISNODE may get access.

That's the kind of fragility I was trying to avoid.


That said, in practice, I have found that basic kernel operations don't
generally target use kmalloc_node() w/ __GFP_THISNODE - there's just
nothing to prevent anyone from doing so.

So this seems promising...
And then theres arch/powerpc/platforms/powernv/memtrace.c

static u64 memtrace_alloc_node(u32 nid, u64 size)
{
	... snip ...
        page = alloc_contig_pages(nr_pages, GFP_KERNEL | __GFP_THISNODE |
                                  __GFP_NOWARN | __GFP_ZERO, nid, NULL);
	... snip ...
}

static int memtrace_init_regions_runtime(u64 size)
{
	... snip ...
        for_each_online_node(nid) {
                m = memtrace_alloc_node(nid, size);
	... snip ...
}

static int memtrace_enable_set(void *data, u64 val)
{
	... snip ...
        if (memtrace_init_regions_runtime(val))
                goto out_unlock;
	... snip ...
}

This is the *exact* pattern I said would be hard to police - and it
doesn't look like a bug, just not informed that private nodes exist.

This is why I'm concerned with trying to depend on __GFP_THISNODE as the
filtering function.

That said, the number of __GFP_THISNODE users is very limited
kernel-wide, so maybe that's an acceptable maintenance burden?

~Gregory

series_items.subject: [PATCH 01/19] nfs/localio: fix nfsd_file ref leak on nfs_local_doio() init failure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a reference leak in the NFS server's localio code, specifically in the nfs_local_doio() function. The leak occurs when certain error paths are taken, causing the localio (nfsd_file) reference to be held indefinitely. The patch introduces additional cleanup code to release these references and prevent namespace teardown issues.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Raised a question about the accuracy of the Fixes: tag and suggested checking the commit history. Also asked whether the patch should be sent through specific trees due to its impact on NFS server behavior.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jun 9, 2026, at 1:47 PM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> Two early return paths in nfs_local_doio() fail to release the localio
> (nfsd_file) reference passed in by the caller:
>
> - When hdr->args.count is zero, the function returns 0 without calling
>   nfs_local_file_put().
>
> - When nfs_local_iocb_init() fails (e.g. -ENOMEM from allocation or
>   -EOPNOTSUPP if the file lacks read_iter/write_iter), the function
>   returns the error without releasing localio or completing the hdr
>   lifecycle.
>
> A leaked nfsd_file pins the associated net namespace reference,
> blocking network namespace teardown, and holds a reference on the
> exported filesystem, preventing unmount.
>
> Fix the zero-count path by adding the missing nfs_local_file_put()
> call. Fix the iocb init failure path by jumping to a new cleanup label
> that releases localio, sets hdr->task.tk_status, and calls
> nfs_local_hdr_release() -- matching the existing error handling pattern
> for the post-iocb error path.
>
> Fixes: e77c464c31b3 ("nfs/nfsd: add "local io" support")

I don't seem to have commit e77c464c31b3 in my tree. Should this be

  Fixes: 70ba381e1a43 ("nfs: add LOCALIO support")

?

> Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  fs/nfs/localio.c | 16 +++++++++++++---
>  1 file changed, 13 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)

This updates only a client-side source file. Should it go through
Anna/Trond's trees? Or were you thinking that, since the leak
impacts only NFS server behavior, I should take it?


-- 
Chuck Lever

series_items.subject: [PATCH 02/19] nfsd: clear opcnt on compound arg release to prevent OOB read
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server where stale compound metadata could be exposed to userspace through the status interface. The issue occurs when an error path is taken after setting the RPC status counter to an odd value, causing an out-of-bounds read from adjacent slab memory. To prevent this, the patch clears the operation count field (opcnt) in the nfsd4_release_compoundargs function when releasing compound arguments.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 03/19] nfsd: add missing read barrier to rpc_status_get dumpit seqcount retry
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a missing read barrier to the rpc_status_get_dumpit function in nfsd, ensuring that all data reads complete before re-checking the status counter on weakly-ordered architectures. Without this barrier, concurrent modifications by the writer could cause the retry logic to be ineffective. The patch matches the barrier semantics of the standard seqcount read-side.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 04/19] nfsd: fix netlink dumpit error handling for rpc_status_get
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes an error handling issue in the NFS daemon's netlink dump functionality. When composing a message for rpc_status_get, the code would return -ENOBUFS on failure to add attributes without canceling the partial message, causing the netlink dump infrastructure to abort the entire dump. The patch corrects this by canceling the partial message and returning the number of bytes added if some messages were successfully added, allowing for pagination instead of a fatal error.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 05/19] sunrpc: defer rq_argp and rq_resp free until after RCU grace period
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free bug in the sunrpc code by deferring the freeing of rq_argp and rq_resp until after an RCU grace period, ensuring that any RCU readers have finished accessing these resources before they are freed. The patch introduces a new call_rcu callback to free these resources asynchronously, alongside the existing kfree_rcu for the svc_rqst struct.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 06/19] nfsd: check nfsd4_acl_to_attr() return value in nfsd4_create()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server code where ACL translation errors were being silently discarded during file creation. The patch adds an early exit check after nfsd4_acl_to_attr() to ensure that any errors are properly handled and do not cause the CREATE operation to proceed without the requested ACL.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 07/19] nfsd: add filehandle match check to nfsd4_delegreturn()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a filehandle match check to the nfsd4_delegreturn() function in the NFSv4 state machine. The function previously did not verify that the delegation's file matches the current file handle, allowing a client to potentially destroy the correct delegation and wake up the wrong inode's waiters. The patch introduces an additional call to nfs4_check_fh() after generation check to ensure filehandle matching.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 08/19] nfsd: validate nseconds in TIME_DELEG decode paths
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds range checks to prevent a malformed timespec from being stored in the Linux kernel's NFS server (nfsd). The checks ensure that the 'nseconds' field, which represents nanoseconds, is less than NSEC_PER_SEC. This fix addresses a potential issue where a malformed timespec could propagate through notify_change() to disk. The patch modifies two files: nfs4callback.c and nfs4xdr.c, adding range checks in both SETATTR and CB_GETATTR paths. The changes were made to address an issue introduced by the 'nfsd: add support for delegated timestamps' commit (6ae30d6eb26b).

series_items.subject: [PATCH 09/19] nfsd: remove premature NFS4_OO_CONFIRMED in CLAIM_PREVIOUS path
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes a premature setting of the NFS4_OO_CONFIRMED flag in the CLAIM_PREVIOUS path of the nfsd kernel module. The flag is set too early, causing it to remain permanently confirmed even if an open operation fails. The correct success-path setter already exists in init_open_stateid(). This change only affects pre-NFSv4.1 sessions and fixes a bug introduced by a previous patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 10/19] nfsd: fix version mismatch loops in nfsd_acl_init_request()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the nfsd_acl_init_request() function where the supported version range for PROG_MISMATCH is incorrectly calculated due to a version mismatch between rqstp->rq_vers and the loop variable i. The fix involves replacing rqstp->rq_vers with the loop variable i, matching the pattern used by the sibling nfsd_init_request() function. This patch addresses an issue introduced in commit e333f3bbefe3 ('nfsd: Allow containers to set supported nfs versions') and was assisted by Claude.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 11/19] nfsd: fix FL_SLEEP being set unconditionally for all LOCK types
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server code where the FL_SLEEP flag was being set unconditionally for all lock types, including non-blocking ones. The issue was caused by a bitmask test that was introduced in a previous commit. This patch replaces the bitmask test with explicit equality checks to ensure that the FL_SLEEP flag is only set when necessary.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 12/19] nfsd: add fh_want_write() for early-verified SETATTR in nfsd_proc_setattr()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a call to fh_want_write() in the nfsd_proc_setattr() function, which is necessary because the early verification of SETATTR causes the mount write reference to be skipped. The missing call was introduced by a previous change that disabled an NFSv2 timestamp workaround for NFSv3+. This patch ensures that notify_change() runs with a valid mount write reference.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 13/19] nfsd: fix clock domain mismatch in clients_still_reclaiming()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server where clients can hold the server in grace indefinitely by sending CLAIM_PREVIOUS OPEN requests, blocking all non-reclaim operations for other clients. The issue arises from a clock domain mismatch between CLOCK_REALTIME and CLOCK_BOOTTIME used in the deadline computation of the clients_still_reclaiming() function. To fix this, the patch adds a new boot_time_bt field to store the boot time in CLOCK_BOOTTIME and uses it alongside the existing boot_time for the deadline computation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 14/19] nfsd: use test_and_clear_bit for somebody_reclaimed to prevent lost update
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a potential issue in the NFS daemon where a concurrent set operation could be lost due to a race condition between testing and clearing a flag. The patch replaces separate test_bit() and clear_bit() calls with a single atomic test_and_clear_bit() call, ensuring that the read-and-clear operation is atomic and preventing lost updates.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 15/19] nfsd: reject reclaim LOCK after RECLAIM_COMPLETE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFSv4 server where it incorrectly accepts reclaim LOCK requests after RECLAIM_COMPLETE has been sent by a client. The fix adds a check to ensure that the per-client NFSD4_CLIENT_RECLAIM_COMPLETE bit is set before accepting a reclaim LOCK request, as required by RFC 8881. This prevents the server from accepting invalid reclaim requests and ensures data consistency.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 16/19] nfsd: validate sockaddr length per family in listener_set
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS daemon's netlink listener set functionality by validating the length of sockaddr structures per address family. The issue arose from an overly permissive policy that allowed a 16-byte NFSD_A_SOCK_ADDR with sa_family=AF_INET6, causing an out-of-bounds read across multiple consumers. To fix this, the patch tightens the policy to require at least 16 bytes for NFSD_A_SOCK_ADDR and adds per-family length validation in both nlmsg_for_each_attr_type loops.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 17/19] lockd, nfsd: RCU-protect nlmsvc_ops dispatch
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a use-after-free bug in the lockd and nfsd modules by RCU-protecting the nlmsvc_ops dispatch. The bug occurs when the nfsd module is removed, causing stale loads of the nlmsvc_ops pointer. To fix this, the patch declares nlmsvc_ops as __rcu, publishes it via rcu_assign_pointer(), and clears it via RCU_INIT_POINTER() + synchronize_rcu(). Additionally, a struct module *owner field is added to nlmsvc_binding and the module is pinned across indirect calls using try_module_get/module_put. This ensures that the binding is properly torn down when the module is removed, avoiding leaks of struct file references.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 18/19] nfsd: move nfsd_debugfs_init() after nfsd4_init_slabs() in init_nfsd()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the initialization of debugfs files in the NFS daemon to after slab allocation has succeeded, preventing orphaned debugfs files when slab allocation fails.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 19/19] nfsd: initialize DRC hash table before registering shrinker
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug on weakly-ordered architectures where the DRC hash table is not fully initialized before it becomes visible to the shrinker, causing a NULL dereference. The fix involves moving the initialization of the bucket list heads and the drc_hashsize store before registering the shrinker. This ensures that the hash table is fully initialized before it can be accessed by the shrinker.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series contains 19 bugfixes for the NFS daemon (nfsd) found via LLM inspection, addressing issues such as reference leaks, incorrect handling of compound arguments and read barriers, premature confirmation of locks, and validation errors in various paths. The patches aim to improve the stability and correctness of nfsd, particularly in scenarios that may not be easily triggerable or require specific conditions.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about nfsd_file reference leaks in the nfs_local_doio() function, specifically two early return paths that failed to release the localio reference. The author agreed to fix this by adding a missing nfs_local_file_put() call and restructuring the error handling path to match an existing pattern.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about stale compound metadata being exposed through the RPC status dumpit handler, explained that reading indices 8-15 of args->ops[] accesses adjacent slab memory and leaks it to userspace via netlink, and agreed to unconditionally set opcnt to 0 in nfsd4_release_compoundargs() so this issue is fixed.
review_comments.raw_body:
nfsd4_release_compoundargs() resets args->ops to the inline iops[8]
array when the dynamically-allocated ops buffer is freed, but leaves
args->opcnt at its original value (which can be up to 200 for NFSv4.1+
compounds).

If rq_status_counter is stuck at an odd value (which can happen when
nfsd_dispatch() hits an error path after setting it odd), the RPC
status dumpit handler reads min(opcnt, 16) entries from args->ops[].
Since iops only has 8 elements and is the last field in struct
nfsd4_compoundargs, reading indices 8-15 accesses adjacent slab memory
and leaks it to userspace via netlink.

Zero opcnt unconditionally in nfsd4_release_compoundargs() so stale
compound metadata is never exposed through the status interface.

Fixes: bd9d6a3efa97 ("NFSD: add rpc_status netlink support")
Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8
Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)

diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c b/fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c
index b9037d99b564..1e4a51926910 100644
--- a/fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c
+++ b/fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c
@@ -6440,6 +6440,7 @@ void nfsd4_release_compoundargs(struct svc_rqst *rqstp)
 		args->ops = args->iops;
 		kvfree_rcu_mightsleep(old_ops);
 	}
+	args->opcnt = 0;
 	while (args->to_free) {
 		struct svcxdr_tmpbuf *tb = args->to_free;
 		args->to_free = tb->next;

-- 
2.54.0

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the missing read memory barrier in nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit() on weakly-ordered architectures, agreeing to add smp_rmb() before the second smp_load_acquire() to match the barrier semantics of the standard seqcount read-side.
review_comments.raw_body:
The hand-rolled seqcount-like protocol in nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit()
is missing a read memory barrier (smp_rmb) before its second counter
check.  The standard kernel read_seqcount_retry() includes smp_rmb()
to ensure that all data reads complete before the counter is re-checked.

Without this barrier, on weakly-ordered architectures (ARM, POWER),
the CPU may reorder field reads past the second counter check, making
the retry logic ineffective: it could observe a consistent counter pair
while reading fields that have been concurrently modified by the writer.

Add smp_rmb() before the second smp_load_acquire() to match the
barrier semantics of the standard seqcount read-side.

Fixes: ac18892ea3f7 ("NFSD: add rpc_status netlink support")
Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8
Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

 fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c | 6 ++++--
 1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c b/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
index c06d25c06f06..a4b5b1467fe2 100644
--- a/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
+++ b/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
@@ -1576,9 +1576,11 @@ int nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit(struct sk_buff *skb,
 #endif /* CONFIG_NFSD_V4 */
 
 			/*
-			 * Acquire rq_status_counter before reporting the rqst
-			 * fields to the user.
+			 * Ensure all field reads complete before re-checking
+			 * the status counter. Pairs with the smp_store_release
+			 * in nfsd_dispatch to form a seq-lock like protocol.
 			 */
+			smp_rmb();
 			if (smp_load_acquire(&rqstp->rq_status_counter) !=
 			    status_counter)
 				continue;

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about use-after-free in svc_rqst_free() by moving the kfree of rq_argp and rq_resp into an explicit call_rcu() callback, ensuring that RCU readers do not access freed resources.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about silently discarding ACL translation errors in nfsd4_create() by adding an early exit check after nfsd4_acl_to_attr(). The fix matches the pattern used in nfsd4_setattr().
review_comments.raw_body:
nfsd4_create() stores the return value of nfsd4_acl_to_attr() in
status, but the switch(create->cr_type) block unconditionally
overwrites it in every branch. ACL translation errors are silently
discarded, and the CREATE proceeds without the requested ACL.

Add an early exit check after nfsd4_acl_to_attr(), matching the
pattern already used in nfsd4_setattr().

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the potential for a client to DELEGRETURN with a mismatched filehandle, which could destroy the correct delegation and wake the wrong inode's waiters. The author agreed to add a missing nfs4_check_fh() call after the generation check in the nfsd4_delegreturn() function.
review_comments.raw_body:
nfsd4_delegreturn() is the only stateful NFSv4 operation that does
not call nfs4_check_fh() to verify the delegation's file matches
cstate->current_fh. A client can DELEGRETURN with a mismatched
filehandle, destroying the correct delegation but waking the wrong
inode's waiters.

Add the missing nfs4_check_fh() call after the generation check.

Fixes: 1da177e4c3f4 ("Linux-2.6.12-rc2")
Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8
Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

@@ -8079,6 +8079,10 @@ nfsd4_delegreturn(struct svc_rqst *rqstp, struct nfsd4_compound_state *cstate,
 	if (status)
 		goto put_stateid;
 
+	status = nfs4_check_fh(&cstate->current_fh, &dp->dl_stid);
+	if (status)
+		goto put_stateid;
+
 	trace_nfsd_deleg_return(stateid);
 	destroy_delegation(dp);
 	smp_mb__after_atomic();

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledged a bug in nfsd4_open() where the openowner is permanently confirmed if do_open_fhandle() fails, and agreed to remove the premature setter for NFSv4.0.
review_comments.raw_body:
nfsd4_open() sets NFS4_OO_CONFIRMED on the openowner before calling
do_open_fhandle(), which can fail. If it fails, the openowner stays
permanently confirmed despite the OPEN failing. The correct
success-path setter already exists in init_open_stateid().

Remove the premature setter. NFSv4.1+ is unaffected as sessions
always confirm at creation time.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds two new tracepoints to the Linux kernel's mm/lruvec module: mm_lru_add_drain and mm_lru_add_drain_all. The first tracepoint reports when an LRU add batch is drained, including the CPU and number of folios in the batch. This allows tracing to distinguish between full drains and partial drains. The second tracepoint captures callers of __lru_add_drain_all and whether they set the force flag for all CPUs. These additions help attribute LRU lock contention to specific callers and improve debugging capabilities.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/lruvec: trace LRU add drains

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called mm_lru_add_drain to report when an LRU add batch is drained, including the CPU and number of folios added. This allows for better tracing and attribution of full drains versus partial drains, which can help identify sources of contention on the LRU lock. The patch introduces a new function lru_add_drain_cpu that calls the tracepoint when draining an LRU add batch. The change is made to improve debugging and performance analysis capabilities in Linux.

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/lruvec: trace LRU add drains and drain-all queuing

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds two new tracepoints to the Linux kernel's memory management (mm) subsystem: mm_lru_add_drain and mm_lru_drain_all_queue. The first tracepoint reports when an LRU add batch is drained, allowing tracing of partial drains and attribution to calling stacks. The second tracepoint captures when per-CPU drain work is queued for remote drain operations, including the requester stack and target CPU. These additions improve debugging capabilities by providing more detailed information about memory management events.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] firmware: arm_sdei: add SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL support

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called sdei_event_signal() to the Linux kernel's firmware/arm_sdei.c file. This function is a thin wrapper over the SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL call, which signals the software-signalled event (event 0) to another processor element (PE). The function takes two parameters: event_num and mpidr, where mpidr is the target PE's MPIDR affinity. The patch also updates the include/linux/arm_sdei.h file to add a declaration for sdei_event_signal(), and adds a new enum value SDEI_1_0_FN_SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL to the include/uapi/linux/arm_sdei.h file. This change is intended to make it safe to call from NMI or crash context, as it does not take any locks.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 3/3] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 3/3] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds an additional escalation path to the smp_send_stop() function on arm64 architecture, which is used to stop a CPU that has become wedged and is ignoring interrupts. When the regular IPI (Inter-Processor Interrupt) and pseudo-NMI paths fail, the patch triggers an SDEI (System Description Event Interface) NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) as a last resort. This allows the system to recover from the wedged CPU by sending it an SDEI event that will eventually lead to its shutdown. The patch also introduces new functions and modifications to existing code to support this escalation path.

review_comments.author: Doug Anderson
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the patch does not handle the case where the SDEI event is already pending when trying to escalate to it, and requested a check for this condition before triggering the SDEI event.

Reviewer Doug Anderson suggested combining the stop functionality into a single function arm64_nmi_cpu_stop() to avoid synchronization issues across different stop paths, and provided an example implementation.

Reviewer Doug Anderson suggested that the patch could be improved by separately testing for SDEI availability before attempting to use it, and proposed modifying sdei_nmi_stop_cpus() to return void instead of printing a message.

Reviewer Doug Anderson questioned the shared EVENT ID for 'backtrace' and 'stop', suggesting separate IDs would improve code understandability and allow elimination of the sdei_nmi_stop_mask variable.

Reviewer Doug Anderson questioned the use of 'or' instead of 'copy' in sdei_nmi_stop_mask, suggesting a potential issue with the logic
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi,

On Tue, Jun 9, 2026 at 6:58 AM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

Can we combine everything into one function so we don't have to keep
all the different stop functionality in sync? Like this (untested):

void __noreturn arm64_nmi_cpu_stop(struct pt_regs *regs, bool die_on_crash)
{
  unsigned int cpu = smp_processor_id();
  bool crash = IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_KEXEC_CORE) && crash_stop;

  /*
   * Use local_daif_mask() instead of local_irq_disable() to make sure
   * that pseudo-NMIs are disabled. The "stop" code starts with
   * an IRQ and falls back to NMI (which might be pseudo). If the IRQ
   * finally goes through right as we're timing out then the NMI could
   * interrupt us. It's better to prevent the NMI and let the IRQ
   * finish since the pt_regs will be better.
   */
  local_daif_mask();

  if (crash)
    crash_save_cpu(regs, cpu);

  set_cpu_online(cpu, false);

  sdei_mask_local_cpu();

  if (crash && die_on_crash)
    __cpu_try_die(cpu);

  /* just in case */
  cpu_park_loop();
}

Then in do_handle_IPI(), it's just:

  case IPI_CPU_STOP:
  case IPI_CPU_STOP_NMI:
    arm64_nmi_cpu_stop(get_irq_regs(), true);
    break;

...and from the SDEI code you pass "false" for "die_on_crash".

Perhaps when doing that you'd stop unconditionally getting the cpu in
do_handle_IPI() and just call it for `IPI_KGDB_ROUNDUP` since it would
now be the only consumer of that local variable.

FWIW, I'm not totally sure I followed the logic for why "die_on_crash"
needs to be "false" for the SDEI case, but I also am not terribly
familiar with KEXEC and crash-dumping kernels, since I've never
attempted to get that feature to work.

---

Perhaps it's being a bit pedantic, but it's a little weird that you're
printing a message that sounds like "I'm going to retry with SDEI"
after you've already done it. It feels like it would be nominally
cleaner (and more parellel with the pseudo-NMI case) if you could
separately test if SDEI is available. Then sdei_nmi_stop_cpus() would
just return void?

---

Do you have any reasoning for why you don't pick a separate EVENT ID
for "backtrace" vs. "stop". If you absolutely have to share an ID
because they're a limited resource then I guess it's fine, but it
would make the code easier to understand / reason about if they were
separate IDs.

If you had a separate EVENT ID, then it seems like you could
completely eliminate the (potentially large) `sdei_nmi_stop_mask`
variable, right? Any time a "STOP" event fires you can unconditionally
consider it to be a stop w/ no globals needed, right?

---

As per above, hopefully we can get rid of `sdei_nmi_stop_mask`. ...but
if we keep it, I'm curious why "or" and not "copy"?

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] arm64: cross-CPU NMI via SDEI

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] arm64: cross-CPU NMI via SDEI

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a new mechanism to deliver NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) across CPUs on arm64 architecture, using the SDEI (System Description Event Infrastructure) protocol. The goal is to enable interrupting a CPU that has its interrupts locally masked, which is necessary for certain debug and observability features such as system backtraces and crash dumps. The existing mechanisms, GICv3 pseudo-NMI and FEAT_NMI, have limitations or are not widely supported. This series adds a new delivery backend using SDEI, which incurs no additional cost on the hot path and can reach a CPU spinning with DAIF masked. It does not introduce a new hard-lockup detector but provides a way to reach a stalled CPU for existing detection methods.

[PATCH v2 2/3] drivers/firmware: add SDEI cross-CPU NMI service for arm64

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a feature to deliver NMI-like events to interrupt-masked CPUs on arm64 architecture using the SDEI software-signalled event mechanism, which is more efficient than the pseudo-NMI hot-path cost. The first user of this feature is the arch_trigger_cpumask_backtrace() function, which can now reach masked CPUs without relying on IPIs. A future watchdog backend will also be able to use this feature. The patch introduces a new SDEI cross-CPU NMI service and modifies several files to support it.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 4/4] arm64: route crash_smp_send_stop() last resort through SDEI

version_history.subject: [PATCH 4/4] arm64: route crash_smp_send_stop() last resort through SDEI

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds the SDEI (System Description Event Interface) as a last resort to stop CPUs in an arm64 system when normal methods fail. It signals event 0 at still-online CPUs, which runs crash_save_cpu() on the wedged context and parks them. This approach is chosen because an SDEI-stopped CPU never completes its event, making it less recoverable than a normal stop. The patch adds functionality to handle this scenario and includes changes to various kernel files.

review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the SDEI cross-CPU NMI handler captures crash_save_cpu() state from the wedged context before parking the CPU, but this may not be sufficient to ensure recoverability since an SDEI-stopped CPU never completes its event.

Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the patch adds SDEI as a last resort for crash_smp_send_stop(), but not for regular smp_send_stop(), and suggested modifying smp_send_stop() to fall back to SDEI if sending the NMI failed, instead of adding this separate path.

Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that passing a cpumask_t to the stack in crash_smp_send_stop() can lead to issues due to its potential size under certain CONFIG options, and suggested avoiding this by defining it as static.
